title: Contact Us
description: Contact Rosehill College for payments, reporting absences, student services, enrolment, international students, adult education, the school nurse, and campus location details.
text: Whakapā mai Contact us Front office reception is open 8.00am to 4.00pm — phone 09 295 0661 . For anything else, the right contact is below. Report an absence — 0800 722 929 Ngā tari Who to contact Front Office Reception Office hours 8.00am – 4.00pm Phone 09 295 0661 Absences Free call 0800 RCAWAY (0800 722 929) Email attendance@rosehillcollege.school.nz Or report via the SchoolBridge app Accounts Office Email accounts@rosehillcollege.school.nz Student Office Email studentoffice@rosehillcollege.school.nz School Nurse Phone 09 295 0661 (ext 913) Email nurses@rosehillcollege.school.nz Enrolment Office Mr Loong — phone 09 295 0661 (ext 871) Email enrolments@rosehillcollege.school.nz International Student Department Phone 09 295 0661 (ext 824 or 884) Email internationaldepartment@rosehillcollege.school.nz 24-hour emergency line 027 463 0267 Adult & Community Education The 2026 programme is now available — Term 1 courses start in early February. rosehilladultlearn.co.nz · 09 636 9060 · ace@rosehillcollege.school.nz Staff members Contact details for teaching staff are on Our Staff . Ngā utu Payments to Rosehill College Payments to Rosehill College can be made by cash, EFTPOS (at the student office), or internet banking. Bank account details Account name: Rosehill College Board Account number: 12-3031-0837472-000 When making an online payment, please include the following details in your reference fields to ensure it is correctly allocated: Student name Student ID Reference — e.g. "Camp Fees", "Trip", "Uniform" Email: accounts@rosehillcollege.school.nz Ngā ngaronga Absences — procedures for parents Our staff use the Electronic Attendance Register (eAR) for real-time tracking of student attendance. Early Notification sends a text message to parents of students whose non-attendance is unexplained, and attendance is visible 24/7 on the password-protected web portal. Reporting an absence If your student is absent from school, please phone the Attendance Officer on 295 0661 ext 869 or 0800 RCAWAY (0800 722 929) at any time and leave a message. You can also report via the SchoolBridge app, or email attendance@rosehillcollege.school.nz . If you receive a text message notifying of an absence: reply to the text, phone our Attendance Officer, or provide a note explaining the student's absence. Leaving school during the day Please provide a note for your student to take to their Head of House at the start of the day. The student will obtain an 'Exit Permit' which they present at the Student Office before they leave the school grounds. Year 9–12 students may not leave the school grounds without permission. We need to know where students are at all times. If a student is unwell at school The student should go to the nurse with permission of the teacher. Students are not permitted to privately contact their parents and leave the school grounds without clearance from school staff. Arriving late Late to the first period: the student must go straight to their class to sign in late and provide a note to their Manaakitia Mai teacher. Late after Period 1: the student must sign in at the Student Office and provide a note from the parent/caregiver advising the reason — or the parent/caregiver should phone the Attendance Officer and leave a message. Long-term absence (more than four days) If the student will be absent during term time for an extended period — please advise date of absence, return date and reason for absence by one of the following methods: Phone the Attendance Officer on 295 0661 ext 869 or 0800 722 929 ; or Send a letter addressed to Principal, Rosehill College, PO Box 72546, Papakura 2244; or Email attendance@rosehillcollege.school.nz . He pātai tāu? Send us a message For enrolment, international student or general enquiries. Prefer email? General enquiries: inquiries@rosehillcollege.school.nz Enrolment: enrolments@rosehillcollege.school.nz International: internationaldepartment@rosehillcollege.school.nz Kei hea mātau Find us 5 Edinburgh

title: Alternative Education
description: Rosehill College leads the Alternative Education (Alt Ed) Programme for the Papakura area, providing flexible, supportive learning pathways for students in Years 9–11 in partnership with Blue Light Papakura and Te Awatea.
text: Alt Ed Alternative Education Programme Rosehill College leads the Alternative Education (Alt Ed) Programme for the Papakura area, in partnership with Blue Light Papakura and Te Awatea. About the programme The programme provides flexible, supportive learning pathways for students in Years 9–11 (under 16 years old) who need a tailored environment to succeed. Its purpose is to provide new learning opportunities for students who have become disengaged from mainstream education. This programme is designed for students who have become disengaged from mainstream education and would benefit from a more personalised and supportive learning environment. Enrolments Email: alted@rosehillcollege.school.nz Phone: (09) 295 0661 ext 871 Lead support Mr J Fraser Email: j.fraser@rosehillcollege.school.nz Enrolment process 1. Referral or enquiry Contact the Alt Ed team or speak with your Kaitiaki. 2. Submit enrolment form Complete and return the Alternative Education Enrolment Form. 3. Initial interview Student, whānau, and referring school meet with the Alt Ed team. 4. Transition plan A personalised learning and attendance plan is developed. Programme focus Core learning: literacy, numeracy, and NCEA Foundation Level Pathways: transition planning, employment skills, and work readiness Life skills: hauora, resilience, communication, and teamwork Service and leadership: building social responsibility and whanaungatanga Support: small class sizes, mentoring, and wrap-around pastoral care Programme options (2026) The offsite programmes offer pick-ups and drop-offs, breakfast and lunch, and access to facilities including a fitness centre, music recording, and outdoor education. Alternative Education programme options for 2026 Provider Focus Rosehill College (on site) Re-engagement, foundation learning, transition to school Blue Light Papakura Youth development and leadership Papakura Activity Centre (Te Awatea) Holistic, whānau-based learning and wellbeing support Expected outcomes The programme aims to support students to: Re-engage in education Improve attendance and engagement Develop literacy, numeracy, and communication skills Build confidence, resilience, and self-motivation Transition into further education, training, or employment Downloads Find out more about the programme in our information guide. Alternative Education information guide PDF Apply now To apply, please complete the Alternative Education Enrolment Form and return it to the Alt Ed team at alted@rosehillcollege.school.nz. Email the Alt Ed team Contact us

title: Course Information
description: Explore detailed course information at Rosehill College to find the right path for your educational journey. Learn about our diverse curriculum offerings, subject choices, and course requirements designed to support student interests, strengths, and future goals.
text: Curriculum Course information At Rosehill College, we believe in providing a balanced education that not only focuses on academic excellence but also fosters personal growth and development. Our courses are structured to challenge students intellectually while supporting their individual learning needs. Finding the right pathway Here, you will find detailed descriptions of the diverse range of courses we offer, designed to cater to the varied interests and aspirations of our students. Our curriculum is comprehensive and inclusive, ensuring that every student has the opportunity to pursue their passions and achieve their academic goals. Do you need assistance in identifying a pathway? Are you uncertain about the course requirements for your university or trade? Are you feeling overwhelmed by the options available? Visit our careers advisor at their office, located in the library. Alternatively, you may email them at careers@rosehillcollege.school.nz . Careers advisor Office in the library careers@rosehillcollege.school.nz Offered courses At Rosehill College, we strive to create a dynamic and supportive learning environment where junior students can thrive academically, socially, and personally. We are also committed to providing a rich and diverse educational experience that equips our senior students with the knowledge, skills, and confidence to succeed in their future endeavours. Our experienced and dedicated teachers are committed to guiding students through these formative years, ensuring they are well-prepared for next steps in their academic and professional journeys. Junior courses (Year 9–11) Our junior curriculum is designed to provide a comprehensive and engaging educational experience for Year 9, Year 10 and Year 11 students. We aim to lay a solid foundation for future academic success and personal growth by offering a wide variety of subjects that cater to diverse interests and abilities. Year 9 courses 2026 PDF Year 10 courses 2026 PDF Year 11 courses 2026 PDF Senior courses (Year 12 and 13) Our senior curriculum is designed to provide Year 12 and Year 13 students with specialised and advanced learning opportunities that prepare them for higher education, vocational training, and successful careers. We offer a broad range of subjects, allowing students to tailor their education to their interests and aspirations. Year 12 & 13 NCEA course information 2026 PDF Need help choosing your courses? Our careers advisor is based in the library and can help you identify a pathway, check university or trade requirements, and make sense of the options. Email the careers advisor Contact the college

title: Digital Learning and BYOD
description: Digital learning at Rosehill College — BYOD guidance, recommended Chromebook specifications, our Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement, and cyber safety resources for whānau.
text: Digital learning Digital learning and BYOD At Rosehill College, we are committed to leveraging digital technologies to enhance our students' learning experiences. As part of this commitment, all students are recommended to have a personal digital device, which they will use to access educational resources, collaborate with peers, and engage in interactive learning activities. Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement To ensure a safe and effective digital learning environment, we require all parents and caregivers to review and agree to our Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement before their child is enrolled at Rosehill College. This agreement outlines the responsibilities and expectations for both students and parents regarding the use of digital devices and online behaviour. By adhering to these guidelines, we aim to provide a secure and productive digital learning environment that supports our students' educational growth and prepares them for the future. Please take the time to read through the Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement to understand the policies and procedures in place to protect and enhance your child's digital learning experience at Rosehill College. Rosehill College digital usage guidelines Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement Google Drive Phones away for the day Ministry of Education PDF E-learning and choosing a device At Rosehill College, all students benefit from the additional learning opportunities that a digital device provides, making it a necessary learning tool. Each student's device will need to connect to the Rosehill College wireless network, and some devices will be better suited to this task than others. Rosehill College recommends a Chromebook as a functional and affordable tool that gives students access to Google applications, which teachers use to manage resources, lessons, and assessments. Other devices, such as laptops and tablets, will also work within our IT infrastructure. Recommended Chromebook specifications The following minimum specifications for a Chromebook are recommended. Recommended minimum Chromebook specifications Specification Recommended minimum Memory size 2GB Screen size 11.6 inches Screen resolution 1366×768 SSD or HDD capacity 16GB Operating system Chrome OS The Google tools students use Gmail Email service Classroom Software for receiving and submitting classwork Drive Cloud-based office productivity software (documents, spreadsheets, calendar, forms) Meet Online video conferences Sites Website creation software YouTube Video service eLearning at Rosehill College Chromebooks are browser-based devices that provide users with access to the web through the Google Chrome browser, allowing all teachers and students to access the suite of Google tools. The New Zealand Curriculum explicitly states that our vision is to foster young people who will be "confident, connected, actively involved and lifelong learners" (NZC, 2007). The Technology learning area within the New Zealand Curriculum strengthens the positioning of digital technologies to ensure that all learners have the opportunity to become digitally capable individuals. This provides a greater focus on students building their skills so they can be innovative creators of digital solutions, moving beyond solely being users and consumers of digital technologies. Cyber security At Rosehill College, we understand the importance of providing a safe and secure digital environment for our students while also harnessing the vast educational resources available online. As parents, we encourage you to actively engage in promoting cyber safety awareness. To assist you in this endeavour, we have compiled a comprehensive list of tools and tips to protect your children online. Here is the link . Furthermore, we strongly recommend familiarising yourselves with cyber safety issues through resources such as Netsafe , a New Zealand organisation dedicated to educating and safeguarding against online threats. Visiting their website is highly recommended. Your

title: Education Outside The Classroom
description: Education Outside the Classroom (EOTC) at Rosehill College — outdoor education at the Waharau Outdoor Education Centre, plus Year 10 Camp dates, forms and payment details.
text: EOTC Education outside the classroom New Zealand's stunning landscapes offer endless opportunities for personal growth, connection with others, and self-discovery. At Rosehill College, we embrace the transformative power of nature through our comprehensive Education Outside the Classroom (EOTC) programme. A proud tradition of outdoor education With a proud tradition of outdoor education, we are fortunate to have ongoing access to the Waharau Outdoor Education Centre, nestled in the breathtaking Hunua Ranges. This exceptional facility serves as a base for various outdoor experiences that enrich our students' lives. But the adventure does not stop there! We offer a wide range of EOTC experiences tailored to accommodate students of all skill levels and confidence. Whether it is hiking, kayaking, rock climbing, or team-building activities, our programmes are designed to engage, inspire, and empower every student. Puni tau tekau Year 10 Camp Each year, our Year 10 students embark on an unforgettable camp experience, led by our enthusiastic Year 12 leaders. Many students highlight these camps as some of their most cherished memories at Rosehill College, where friendships are forged, challenges are embraced, and lifelong skills are developed. 2026 camp dates The Year 10 Camp dates for each House are as follows. Year 10 Camp dates 2026 by House Houses Camp dates Atawhai & Rangatahi 16 – 18 March Pounamu & Taikura 23 – 25 March Kahurangi & Manutaki 25 – 27 March Camp forms Below are the Year 10 Camp forms each student will need to have completed: (House) Parental Consent EOTC Health Profile Form For your information there are also the following: Year 10 Camp Letter Gear List Atawhai Parental Consent Kahurangi Parental Consent Manutaki Parental Consent Pounamu Parental Consent Rangatahi Parental Consent Taikura Parental Consent EOTC Health Profile Camp payments Camp payments can be made via bank transfer using the details below. Please ensure all payment details are entered correctly so that the payment can be allocated to the correct student. Bank ASB Bank Account name Rosehill College Board Account number 12-3031-0837472-00 Particulars Student ID number Code Student's surname Reference Y10 Camp Questions about Year 10 Camp? Get in touch and we can help with forms, gear lists and payment details. Contact us Life at Rosehill College

title: Junior Diploma
description: The Rosehill College Junior Diploma is our in-house qualification for Year 9, 10 and 11 students, recognising participation, effort and achievement across school life.
text: Years 9–11 Junior Diploma At Rosehill College, we are committed to providing a balanced education that fosters both academic excellence and personal growth. As part of this commitment, we have introduced the Rosehill College Junior Diploma for our Year 9, 10, and 11 students. An in-house qualification with clear goals The Junior Diploma serves as an in-house qualification that fills the gap left by the removal of NCEA Level 1, giving students clear goals to strive for in their early high school years. It is designed to encourage students to embrace our school values, build foundational skills in literacy and numeracy, and prepare for the challenges of senior secondary education. Through the Junior Diploma, students experience a structured framework that rewards their participation, effort, and achievement across all aspects of school life, laying the groundwork for future success. Structure and criteria The Junior Diploma recognises students' achievements across four key areas based on Rosehill College's values: Whanaungatanga (Together we are more) – Encourages school participation through house activities, events, and challenges. Higher engagement leads to higher diploma levels. Manaakitanga (We rise by lifting others) – Promotes respect and cultural involvement. Students participate in haka practice, Manaakitia Mai discussions, and school/community groups. Hihiritanga (We are resilient and persevere) – Focuses on attendance, goal-setting, and academic skills. Students complete our Career Central programme, improve literacy and numeracy, and demonstrate perseverance. Rangatiratanga (Excellence in all that we do) – Recognises academic success. Students accumulate academic points from assessments, with higher diploma levels requiring more points and stronger performance. Achievement levels Achieved Meets all base requirements. Merit Requires higher participation, attendance, and academic performance. Excellence Requires outstanding involvement, leadership, and top academic results. Yearly breakdown Each year level has specific expectations within these categories, ensuring students develop skills progressively. The Junior Diploma is a progressive programme across Year 9, Year 10, and Year 11, with each year building on the previous one. While the core values and categories remain consistent, the specific expectations grow as students mature. Below is an overview of what the Junior Diploma entails at each year level. Year 9 Junior Diploma PDF Year 10 Junior Diploma PDF Year 11 Junior Diploma PDF Future pathways The Junior Diploma prepares students for senior NCEA study and career planning, ensuring they develop essential skills for success. Strong foundation for NCEA – Students enter Year 12 with foundational literacy, numeracy, and study habits, making the transition to NCEA Level 2 smoother. The programme includes NCEA Literacy and Numeracy corequisites, reducing future workload. Career planning and goal-setting – Through our Career Central programme, students explore career options, set learning goals, and plan their Year 12 subjects accordingly. Leadership and responsibility – Encourages participation in clubs, sports, and leadership roles, helping students develop teamwork, time management, and initiative — valuable traits for tertiary study and employment. Early NCEA credits – Year 11 students earn Level 2 Unit Standards (11 credits), providing a head start on NCEA Level 2. Guidance and support – Teachers and our career advisor help students refine their study plans, explore scholarships, and prepare for their future education or vocational pathways. Ultimately, the Rosehill College Junior Diploma prepares students for life beyond junior high school. It ensures they are academically competent and have the qualifications needed to progress, it instils in them the importance of participation and community, and it sets them on a path of thoughtful career planning. By the end of Year 11, our students have not only earned a di

title: Learning Areas and Subjects
description: Explore the learning areas and subjects at Rosehill College, from English, the Arts and Humanities to Languages, Mathematics, Science, Te Reo Māori, Physical Education and Health, and Technology.
text: Ngā wāhanga ako Learning areas and subjects Our curriculum spans English, the Arts, Humanities, Languages, Mathematics, Physical Education and Health, Science, Te Reo Māori, Technology and ESOL. The Arts Visual arts Year 9: In both the Year 9 courses — Art and Design, students will have the opportunity to explore some art-making conventions, learn about and apply knowledge of elements of art and develop competence within the essential skills through the use of materials and processes. Drawing is the primary means for students to develop and create art works, developing and revisiting their ideas using various approaches in response to a variety of motivations, observations, and imagination, supported by the study of artists' work. In Art there is an emphasis on working within the fields of painting and printmaking, and in Design an emphasis on the fields of sculpture and design, although students will explore aspects of all four fields in both courses. Year 10: In Art and Design, students continue to develop and extend their visual, literate and technical skills within a variety of themes. Drawing is used to underpin the exploration of a variety of two and three dimensional media, encouraging students to pursue and challenge their own creative processes through learning about art, communicating visual ideas and making original work. The courses provide a solid foundation of skill-building in preparation for entry into Level 1 Art History, Visual Art and Photography and Design the following year. Year 11: Year 11 students can study either Visual Arts or Art Design. These courses build on and extend the skills taught in Year 10 Art and Year 10 Art Design. Year 12: Year 12 courses are offered in Art Design, Painting, Printmaking and Drawing, and Photography. These courses build on the skills taught in Year 11 Photography and Design and Visual Arts. Students may take up to two of these practical courses on successful completion of either Year 11 Art course. The Arts Dance Dance students integrate thinking, moving and feeling. They explore and use dance elements, vocabularies, processes and technologies to express personal, group and cultural identities. Students develop literacy in Dance as they learn about and develop skills in performing, choreographing and responding to a variety of genres from a range of historical and contemporary contexts. The Arts Music At Rosehill College we offer Music courses to cater for all! Free weekly lessons are available for Music students, but there may be a waiting list. Check out the instruments that you can learn here at Rosehill College: flute, oboe, clarinet, bass clarinet, saxophone, cornet, trumpet, horn, trombone, tuba, piano, vocals, guitar, bass guitar, and drumkit. Enjoy your musical adventure! The Arts Instrumental and vocal groups Come up to the Music Department to see how you can join any of these extra-curricular Music groups: Concert Band Stage Band Music Tour School Musical Vocal groups Jazz Ensemble Tangata Beats groups Smokefree RockQuest groups Junior Prize Giving Band "Ah, music," he said, wiping his eyes. "A magic beyond all we do here!" J.K. Rowling, Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone Why music matters Why is Music important? This Is Your Brain On Music - How Music Benefits The Brain 25 Remarkable Health Benefits of Singing How playing an instrument benefits your brain - Anita Collins The Arts Drama We only have one request in this programme: be excellent! That means, to yourself, to others, and in everything you do. Be willing to try new things, know that drama can be done by anyone in a supportive and trusting environment. Above all, remember The Golden Rule: you are #3, the work you are doing is #2, everyone else is #1. Drama students express human experience through a focus on role, action and tension, played out in time and space. As students work with drama techniques, they learn to use spoken and written language with increasing control and confidence and to commun

title: Mobile Phone Policy
description: Rosehill College's mobile phone policy — phones away for the day, guidelines for students, consequences for misuse, and advice for parents and caregivers.
text: Phones away for the day Mobile phone policy At Rosehill College, we believe mobile phones are not necessary during the school day. However, we understand that some parents and caregivers want their children to have a phone for after-school communication. Why we have this policy Under the New Zealand Legislation of Education (School Boards) Regulation 2020, the Rosehill College Board has the duty to prohibit the use or access of mobile phones at school. If a student brings a phone to school, the following guidelines apply. This policy helps keep our school a safe, respectful, and focused learning environment. Rosehill College digital usage guidelines Cybersecurity and BYOD Agreement Phones away for the day — whānau FAQs (Ministry of Education) The policy in detail General guidelines The school is not responsible for lost, damaged, or stolen phones. Phones must be on silent and kept in school bags between 8.45am and 3.10pm. Teachers may allow supervised use of phones for learning activities. Phones may be brought on EOTC activities, at the discretion of staff and in line with school policies. In an emergency, parents should contact the school office, and we will pass the message on to students. Parents/caregivers can call or email the Student Office if they need to contact their child during the day. Consequences for misuse If a phone is seen during class or break times, it may be confiscated by a Head of House or any SLT member. It will be kept at the Student Office and collected by a parent, caregiver, or student at the end of the school week (Friday). Phones may be confiscated if they disrupt learning or break school guidelines. Collection is available Fridays between 3.15–4.15pm from the Student Office. Early collection can only be arranged through the Principal by emailing the Principal's Secretary at pa@rosehillcollege.school.nz . Please arrange this in advance. Continued violations may result in a parent/student meeting with a Kaitiaki or Deputy Principal and could be treated as persistent defiance. Inappropriate use during school hours Examples of inappropriate mobile phone use include: Bullying or disrespectful messages about staff or students Taking photos/videos of others without permission Sharing inappropriate content Disrupting learning in class or for others The school will investigate incidents through our discipline procedure. Parents will be informed, and appropriate consequences will be applied depending on the behaviour. Guidelines for parents — use outside of school Parents and caregivers are responsible for their children's phone use outside school hours. We encourage families to: Set clear rules about phone use at home Encourage children to be the same person online and in person Monitor passwords, limit Wi-Fi use overnight, and stay engaged with your child's online activity If online incidents occur If online incidents occur involving Rosehill College students: Reach out to the other student's parents in a calm way to resolve the matter together If behaviour breaches the Harmful Digital Communications Act 2015, please contact Netsafe or the New Zealand Police For further support, resources are available from: Netsafe — www.netsafe.org.nz The Parenting Place — www.parentingplace.nz Need to reach your child during the day? Parents and caregivers can call or email the Student Office, and in an emergency the school office will pass a message on to your child. Contact us Digital learning and BYOD

title: Our Rosehill College Way
description: Discover the Rosehill College Way, including our values of Manaakitanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga, and Hihiritanga. Learn about our expectations for uniform and attendance, including our attendance management plan, and how we foster a positive, inclusive environment that supports student success and personal growth.
text: Values and expectations Our Rosehill College Way Manaakitanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga and Hihiritanga — four values that shape how we connect, reflect, respect and strive every day, alongside clear expectations for attendance and uniform. What do we expect from you? We expect students to attend school every day, as consistent attendance is crucial for developing lifelong learning skills and achieving higher educational outcomes, which contribute to better health, higher incomes, job stability, and stronger, more connected communities. At Rosehill College, we also expect students to be in their uniform as it fosters a sense of belonging and pride within our school community. Wearing a uniform promotes equality, reduces distractions, and helps create a focused learning environment. It reflects our commitment to maintaining high standards and discipline, ensuring that all students present themselves in a respectful and unified manner. Uniforms also prepare students for future professional settings where dress codes are common, instilling a sense of responsibility and readiness for the world beyond school. Uniform expectation Seniors uniform The expectation for students to wear their uniforms at Rosehill College is grounded in the belief that it promotes unity and school spirit. Uniforms eliminate socio-economic disparities, allowing students to focus on their education rather than fashion. Juniors uniform Wearing our uniform supports a disciplined atmosphere and enhances the school's identity, making students easily recognisable as members of our institution. It also encourages students to take pride in their appearance and fosters a professional mindset that benefits them in future academic and career endeavours. Uniform and stationery Every day counts One or two days a week does not seem that much, but the missed learning adds up — and children learn best at the start of the day, so arriving late to school or class matters too. Chart showing how missing one or two days a week adds up over a school year 1 or 2 days a week does not seem that much but... Chart showing the impact of missed school days on learning Chart showing the impact of arriving late to school and class Late for school, late to class? Children learn best at the start of the day. Attendance Management Plan Read our full plan for supporting every student to attend school every day. Attendance Management Plan PDF Manaakitanga Connect "We rise by lifting others" We use mana enhancing, respectful, inclusive words and actions We look after our learning environment We are organised and prepared to learn We bring the equipment we need, including charged laptops/Chromebooks Because: Connecting and learning collectively helps everyone Being prepared for our learning keeps us focused on achieving Everyone has the right to a safe and enjoyable learning environment Rangatiratanga Reflect "Excellence in all that we do" We celebrate success together Knowing what you need to learn to achieve excellence Reflect on my learning and my actions every day to strive to be better Because: We care about learning together My learning is my responsibility and I want to be better We care about self leadership Achieving excellence is everyone's right Whanaungatanga Respect "Together we are more" We arrive on time We wear our uniform correctly We arrive with headphones/phones away We respect others' ideas and contributions — everyone is unique Because: We care about our school community whānau We are ready to greet each other We know phones are not learning devices We care about learning together Our uniform represents commitment to our school community and our readiness to learn Hihiritanga Strive "We are resilient and persevere" We support each other through challenges We follow the 'RHC Way' and encourage others to do the same We know making mistakes are moments of learning We are determined to achieve excellence Because: We encourage personal and collective excellence We care ab

title: Student Support
description: Student support at Rosehill College — Learner Support, Gifted Education, Careers guidance and the Gateway work experience programme.
text: Here to help Student support We are dedicated to fostering a nurturing and inclusive environment for all our students. Our comprehensive support system is designed to address the diverse needs of our students, ensuring they have access to the resources and assistance necessary to thrive both academically and personally. At Rosehill College, we understand that every student is unique, and our dedicated team is here to help. Whether you need academic guidance, mental health support, or assistance with personal challenges, we are committed to providing a safe and supportive space for you to share your concerns. Rosehill College is a KidsLink school. Kidslink connects schools and families with professional services for children and teens. Learner Support The role of Learner Support is to: Assist with transition of students with SEN from year 8 to year 9. Provide support for students with identified Special Educational Needs (SEN) in years 9 to 13. Carry out evaluations of students with suspected SEN. Liaise with parents and caregivers. Provide advice and guidance to teachers on SEN matters. Liaise with support agencies providing services to students with SEN, their caregivers and their teachers. If you have any queries please contact the Head of Learning Support on extension 873. Gifted Education Rosehill College recognises that our student population is diverse. An enriched learning environment is the right of all students and is essential for the development of their gifts and talents. We offer a range of approaches, including advanced courses, acceleration of content delivery, involvement in subject competitions (e.g., Mathex, NSW Science, and the Future Problem Solving National competition), and visits from speakers focusing on goal setting, motivation, philosophy, effective learning strategies, and achieving excellence. The school's high-ability students at the senior level are encouraged to pursue academic excellence and sit for NZQA Scholarship examinations. Depending on the subject area, enrolment in university papers for extension purposes is also available. The College defines giftedness as encompassing various areas of learning, including intellectual, physical, creative, spiritual, and leadership domains. The identification of our gifted students is inclusive and ongoing, using a multi-method approach. These methods include, but are not limited to, teacher observations using characteristic checklists or observation scales, questionnaires, standardised tests (e.g. asTTle), parent referrals, peer referrals, and self-referrals. The most common identification approach is teacher observation and nomination. An elite sports programme is also offered by the College, along with numerous opportunities for involvement in the visual arts, drama, music, and Kapa Haka performances. Gifted Education at Rosehill College Careers The aim of Careers Education at Rosehill College is to provide appropriate career education, information, and guidance for students. The Careers Centre is available for student use before school, during intervals, at lunchtime, and after school, as well as during study time for Year 13 students. It contains information about a wide range of careers and courses and is staffed by Ms Ferguson, the Careers Adviser, and Ms Mowday, the Careers Assistant. Computers with internet access are also available. Students can visit the Careers Room to receive expert advice and assistance to aid in their choices regarding tertiary education and employment opportunities. Assistance is also available for writing CVs, seeking work experience, applying for scholarships or Halls of Residence, and completing applications to tertiary institutions. Additionally, the Careers Department hosts a variety of lunchtime seminars to provide information about tertiary institutions, student loans, Armed Forces, etc. These seminars are advertised in the daily bulletin and on the Careers Bulletin on the school website. If you would like to get i

title: Year 9 SMART Assessment
description: Information for Year 9 whānau about the SMART assessment at Rosehill College — what it is, why it matters, and how students can prepare, including the assessment dates of 4–15 May.
text: Year 9 Year 9 SMART assessment — May 4–15 The SMART (Student Monitoring, Assessment and Reporting Tool) is a new online assessment. It is being introduced across schools in Aotearoa New Zealand from 2026, replacing the e-asTTle assessment. Key details at a glance Dates Monday 4 – Friday 15 May Where Students complete the assessment in the Hall Duration Each assessment typically takes 30–45 minutes Devices Assessments are completed online — students should bring their own device. Parents can let Kaitiaki/Head of House know if a device needs to be booked. Please do so by 4th May. SMART familiarisation site (recommended first step) Students are encouraged to explore the SMART platform before completing the assessment. SMART Familiarisation Site This site allows students to: Try sample questions Become familiar with the assessment format Build confidence before the actual assessment What is the SMART assessment? The SMART (Student Monitoring, Assessment and Reporting Tool) is a new online assessment. It is being introduced across schools in Aotearoa New Zealand from 2026. SMART provides clear and consistent information about student progress in: Reading Writing Mathematics It replaces the e-asTTle assessment and is aligned with the new curriculum. Teachers use SMART results to track student progress and support conversations with students and whānau. Parents and caregivers will receive information about their child's progress through our reporting processes. Purpose of the assessment SMART is a learning check designed to: Help teachers understand what students already know Identify next learning steps Provide information to parents and caregivers The focus is on progress and improvement over time, rather than a single result. Further testing is occurring in Term 4. How the assessment runs Assessments are completed online, students should bring their own device. Each assessment typically takes 30–45 minutes. Questions may include: Multiple choice Short written responses Interactive tasks (for example, drag-and-drop tools) Students complete the assessment in the Hall. Parents can let Kaitiaki/Head of House know if a device needs to be booked. Please do so by 4th May. Further information For more information about SMART, please refer to the official guidance from the Ministry of Education. If you have any questions, please contact the college. SMART familiarisation site Contact the college

title: Welcome to Rosehill College
description: Empowering Year 9–13 students in Papakura, South Auckland to achieve their best. Experience Rosehill College's commitment to academic excellence and personal growth.
text: Mā tātau hangahanga ai te taiao hiranga Through living our values we enable our vision: together we create an environment for personal excellence. Ō tātau uara Our vision and values Rosehill College will nurture a culture of excellence through Hihiritanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga, and Manaakitanga. Manaakitanga Connect "We rise by lifting others." We use mana-enhancing, respectful, inclusive words and actions, and look after our learning environment. Rangatiratanga Reflect "Excellence in all that we do." We celebrate success together, reflect on our learning and our actions every day, and care about self-leadership. Whanaungatanga Respect "Together we are more." We care about our school community whānau, respect others' ideas and contributions, and know everyone is unique. Hihiritanga Strive "We are resilient and persevere." We support each other through challenges, know mistakes are moments of learning, and are determined to achieve excellence. Te noho ki Rosehill Life at Rosehill College Academic excellence, arts and creativity, sports and recreation, wellbeing and support — one community. Rosehill College students enjoying life on campus Our community 1 Rosehill College students focused on their studies Academic excellence 2 Rosehill College students performing in the arts Arts and creativity 1 Rosehill College kapa haka group performing Kapa haka 2 Rosehill College students playing sport Sports and recreation 1 Rosehill College students supported by staff Wellbeing and support 2 The wider Rosehill College community together RHC community 1 Te haerenga The journey — Year 9 to Year 13 Five years, one pathway. From your first day to your last, every step at Rosehill College builds towards personal excellence. 9 Settle in and belong Your Junior Diploma journey begins — our in-house qualification recognising participation, effort and achievement across all aspects of school life — alongside the SMART assessment in literacy and numeracy. Year 9 SMART Assessment 10 Build your foundations The Junior Diploma continues, building foundational skills in literacy and numeracy while you explore a wide variety of subjects — and head beyond the classroom on Year 10 camp. Junior Diploma 11 Step up The final year of the Junior Diploma — awarded at Achieved, Merit or Excellence — sharpens your goals and readies you for NCEA study in the senior school. Course information 12 Specialise NCEA Level 2 and specialised senior courses let you tailor your education to your strengths — with leadership roles opening across sport, arts, culture and service. Senior courses 13 Lead and launch NCEA Level 3, head student leadership, and pathways to university, vocational training and careers — you leave ready for the world beyond school. Our student leaders I tua atu i te akomanga Beyond the classroom Sports, arts, culture and clubs — a co-curricular life with national reach. Rosehill College volleyball players mid-match Volleyball Rosehill College rugby team in action Rugby Rosehill College netball players on court Netball Rosehill College athletes competing in athletics Athletics Rosehill College basketball game Basketball Rosehill College musicians performing Music Rosehill College dance performance Dance Rosehill College touch rugby players Touch Rosehill College softball players Softball Rosehill College weightlifting athlete Weightlifting Explore sports Arts and culture Ko wai mātau Who we are Fly over our campus and see a term at Rosehill College in motion. title: About Us
description: Explore Rosehill College's core values and educational vision.
text: Ko wai mātau Our vision and values Rosehill College, officially opened in 1970, has a proud history of outstanding academic achievement and a record of national success in both sports and the performing arts. Mission and vision Our Mission Statement is: "Together we create an environment for personal excellence." Our Vision Statement is: "Rosehill College will nurture a culture of excellence through Hihiritanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga, and Manaakitanga." Hihiritanga: Strive — we are resilient and persevere Rangatiratanga: Reflect — excellence in all that we do Whanaungatanga: Respect — together we are more Manaakitanga: Connect — we rise by lifting others 1970 Officially opened Year 9–13 Co-educational college 13–18 Ages we serve Papakura Ngāti Tamaoho rohe Mō mātau About us Rosehill College is a co-educational secondary school with a rich history of providing quality education and fostering a supportive learning environment. The College sits within the Ngāti Tamaoho rohe. This area extends from north of the Tamaki Isthmus to south of the Waikato River, to the Whangamarino wetlands. It extends from the West Coast to the Hauraki Gulf/Firth of Thames. Its interests are concentrated in the Manukau lowlands, Āwhitu Peninsula, Papakura, Hunua Ranges, Mangatangi, and Whangamarino. Ngāti Tamaoho has a rich cultural heritage, including traditions, stories, and practices passed down through generations. They have a significant connection to their land and natural resources, and like other iwi, they play an active role in the social, cultural, and economic life of their communities. Rosehill College serves students from Year 9 to Year 13 (ages 13 to 18). The college offers a wide range of academic, sporting, cultural, and extracurricular activities to cater to the diverse interests and needs of its students. Established in 1970, Rosehill College has a rich history of providing quality education and fostering a supportive learning environment. The school emphasises holistic development, aiming to nurture students not only academically but also socially, emotionally, and physically. Rapua ētahi atu Keep exploring Our Rosehill College Way How our four values shape every day at school. Our history More than five decades of Rosehill College, from 1970 to today. Life at Rosehill College Academic excellence, arts, sport, wellbeing and community.

title: Kaupapa Māori
description: Kaupapa Māori at Rosehill College — te ao Māori, kapa haka and our school haka, Haka ko te Kāreti o Pukeroihi.
text: Te ao Māori Kaupapa Māori Kapa haka Haka ko te Kāreti o Pukeroihi (Te koha nunui o Papa Kukupa Tirikatene) Kaea: Kia rite, kia rite, Kia mau! Hii! Kaea: I aa haa haa! Ka eke te wiwi Ka eke te wawa Ka eke te paparangi Hui e I ta eke I ta eke Kaea: Ko Pukeroihi e ngunguru nei I au, au aue ha Kaea: Ko Pukeroihi e ngunguru nei I Au, au aue ha Te koraa o te ahu I ahu mai I ngaa pungarehu o ratau kua haere ra Kaea: A haa Ko Pukeroihi e ngunguru nei I Au au aue haa Kaea: A haa Ko Pukeroihi e ngunguru nei I Au au aue ha Kaea: I aa haa haa! Ka tu te ihi Ihi ka tu te wanawana Ki runga I te rangi e tu iho nei, tu iho nei Hii! Rapua ētahi atu Keep exploring te ao Māori at Rosehill Our values — Hihiritanga, Rangatiratanga, Whanaungatanga and Manaakitanga — shape every day at Rosehill College, from the classroom to the stage. Arts and culture Our vision and values

title: Life At Rosehill College
description: Discover what life is like at Rosehill College! From vibrant extracurricular activities to a supportive learning environment, explore how our students thrive academically, socially, and personally. Join a community focused on growth, creativity, and achievement.
text: Our school Life at Rosehill College Rosehill College offers a vibrant and supportive environment, designed to nurture the growth and development of our students. As one of the leading secondary schools in Auckland, we provide diverse opportunities for all students to excel academically, athletically, and culturally, guiding them to achieve their personal best. Whanaungatanga RHC community and culture At Rosehill College, our House system plays a vital role in fostering a sense of community and school spirit. Each House provides students with the opportunity to contribute, grow as leaders, and celebrate their successes within a supportive and collaborative environment. This system not only encourages healthy competition but also strengthens connections among students, creating lifelong bonds. Our houses Atawhai house emblem Atawhai Kahurangi house emblem Kahurangi Manutaki house emblem Manutaki Pounamu house emblem Pounamu Rangatahi house emblem Rangatahi Taikura house emblem Taikura Academic excellence Academic excellence Rosehill College is renowned for its commitment to academic success. Our dedicated teachers inspire and challenge students to reach their full potential. We offer a comprehensive academic mentoring programme, which includes peer support, targeted tutoring, and leadership development, ensuring that every student is equipped to thrive both in and out of the classroom. Ngā toi Arts and creativity The arts are an integral part of life at Rosehill College, providing students with numerous avenues for self-expression and personal growth. From drama and dance to music and visual arts, our co-curricular programmes are designed to foster creativity and celebrate cultural diversity. Students have the chance to participate in various artistic activities, including school productions, concerts, and cultural festivals, all of which contribute to their holistic development. Ngā hākinakina Sports and recreation Sport plays a significant role in the culture of Rosehill College, promoting physical fitness, teamwork, and school spirit. We offer a wide range of sporting activities for students of all abilities, from competitive sports teams to recreational clubs. Our extensive facilities, including modern gymnasiums and multipurpose courts, provide students with the resources they need to pursue their athletic passions and develop lifelong skills. Hauora Wellbeing and support Student wellbeing is at the heart of everything we do at Rosehill College. We strive to create an inclusive and nurturing environment where all students feel safe, respected, and valued. Our pastoral care system includes dedicated guidance counsellors, peer support programmes, and a focus on student mental health, ensuring that every student has access to the support they need to succeed. Giving back Community engagement Rosehill College believes in the importance of giving back and making a positive impact on the community. Our students are encouraged to engage in various service activities, including volunteering, charity events, and environmental initiatives. These opportunities not only help students develop a sense of social responsibility but also allow them to apply their skills in meaningful ways that benefit others. Rosehill College is more than just a place of learning — it's a community where students are encouraged to grow, explore their interests, and prepare for the future with confidence. Tīmata tō haerenga Come and be part of it Explore our vision and values, or start your enrolment at Rosehill College today. Enrol now About us

title: Our History
description: Learn about the rich history of Rosehill College, where tradition and innovation meet. From our founding to present day, discover how our school has evolved and shaped generations of students. Explore our milestones, values, and commitment to excellence in education.
text: Since 1970 Our history From our first day of attendance on 3 February 1970 to today, Rosehill College has grown alongside its community — more than five decades of milestones, people and achievements. 1970 First day of attendance, 3rd February First tennis courts Canteen opened in A Block Walkathon fundraiser — 20 miles First Board of Governors elected 1975 First Form 7 Tender for opened drama, music and canteen facilities Teacher Tina Bell-Kate, NZ Olympic hockey team Teacher Neisha Pratt, NZ Women's Indoor Cricket World Cup team 2008 Gymnasium/Sports Centre completed Kapa Haka won Mōteatea-Kia Mōhio Kia Rangatiri Trophy at Polyfest Teachers Dave and Lyndon Ormandy left after 22 years Year 9 and 10 teams won Counties Manukau Mathex competition 2015 Concert band received title: Principal's Message
description: Read our Principal's vision for Rosehill College. Learn about our school's values, achievements, and vision for excellence in Auckland education.
text: Te Tumuaki Principal's Message Kia ora, Talofa lava, Fakaalofa lahi atu, Nisa bula vinaka, Kia Orana, Ni Sa, Namaste, Mālō e lelei, Welcome, Herzlich Willkommen, Witamy — greetings and welcome to you all! Grounded in this whenua Rosehill College is a co-educational secondary school that sits within the Ngāti Tamaoho rohe. This area extends from north of the Tamaki Isthmus to south of the Waikato River, to the Whangamarino wetlands. It extends from the West Coast to the Hauraki Gulf/Firth of Thames. Ngāti Tamaoho has a rich cultural heritage, including traditions, stories, and practices passed down through generations. They have a significant connection to their land and natural resources, and like other iwi, they play an active role in the social, cultural, and economic life of their communities. Traditional values, innovative teaching Rosehill College is based on traditional values of Respect, Responsibility and Caring with innovative approaches to teaching and learning that is supported by modern information communication technology. It is our intention to produce graduates that are confident, self-reliant, and have self-respect and respect for others. We celebrate our coeducational and bi-cultural identity and the opportunities presented to establish healthy friendships with people from a diverse range of backgrounds which is Auckland today. For our whānau and parents of Rosehill College I welcome and encourage a strong and active parent network that will support our Values and Vision of the school. Mā tātau hangahanga ai te taiao hiranga Together we create an environment for personal excellence. Davida Suasua — Principal, Rosehill College Tīmata tō haerenga Come and see who we are Explore our values, our history and life at Rosehill College — or start your enrolment today. Enrol now About us

title: Start of School Information
description: Term Dates, Daily Times, Student Handbook, School Buses, School Map, Year 10 Camp, Rosehill College Way, Start of Year Letter to Parents, Stationery List, Uniform, BYOD
text: Te tīmatanga o te tau Start of school information We look forward to welcoming all students back to school in February 2026, and a special welcome to all our new students. Everything whānau need for the first days of the year is here — start dates, daily times, downloads and stationery lists. Start dates 2026 All Year 9 students and those new to Rosehill College will start on Monday 2 February. All other students will begin on Tuesday 3 February. Each group of students is expected to attend school for the whole time on their allocated day. Start of year dates and times 2026 Who Date Times Year 13 re-enrolment Tuesday 27 January 9:00am – 1:00pm New International Students' Orientation Tuesday 27 – Thursday 29 January 9:00am – 2:00pm each day Student Leaders' Camp (at Waharau) Wednesday 28 – Friday 30 January Departs 8:15am Wednesday, returns 5:00pm Friday Year 12 re-enrolment Thursday 29 January 9:00am – 1:00pm Peer Support Leaders Monday 2 February 8:00am – 3:10pm Year 9 and new students only Monday 2 February 8:30am – 3:10pm Buses will be operating. New students who are not Year 9s may leave at 12:50pm. All students on-site together Tuesday 3 February 9:45am – 3:10pm Getting ready for day one Daily times Classes begin at 8:50am, so please ensure your child arrives with time to be in their classroom by the beginning of the school day. Tuesday starts later, with no Period 1. School buses Buses commence their normal runs from the morning of Monday 2 February and pick students up from college at 3:20pm. Manaakitia Mai group Your student's 2026 Manaakitia Mai group is on the emailed version of the start-of-year letter — students need to know this for their first day at school. Picking up students Please arrange to meet your child off site after school. Buses and hundreds of students on foot use the front entrance, so cars cannot come in at pick-up time. Start of year downloads Start of Year Letter to Parents 2026 PDF Student Handbook PDF School map PDF Stationery lists 2026 You may order your stationery online at Officemax Ordering . Year 9 stationery list PDF Year 10 stationery list PDF Year 11 stationery list PDF Year 12 stationery list PDF Year 13 stationery list PDF Everything for the start of the year The essentials for settling into 2026, all in one place. Term dates The full 2026 calendar of terms, holidays and closures. Daily times Bell times for each day of the week, including Tuesday's later start. Rosehill College Way How our four values shape every day at school. School buses Bus routes, timetables and departure times. Uniform Uniform requirements for juniors and seniors, and where to buy. BYOD Digital learning and which device to bring to school. Keep us up to date If any of your details have changed — email address, phone numbers, home address or caregiver and emergency information — please email studentadmin@rosehillcollege.school.nz. Update your details Contact us

title: Yearbook
description: Discover the Rosehill College Yearbook, where cherished memories and milestones come to life. Featuring student achievements, special events, and unforgettable moments, our yearbook is a celebration of the year's journey. Take a look back at the people, places, and experiences that make Rosehill College so special.
text: Celebrating our year Rosehill College yearbook We are excited to offer a fully digital Yearbook experience, allowing you to relive all the memories and highlights of the year online, anytime, and anywhere! Anytime, anywhere In our continued effort to reduce environmental impact and embrace the digital world, the Rosehill College Yearbook is available exclusively online. This ensures easy access to the year's special moments — whether you are at home, at school, or on the go. Browse our yearbooks Rosehill College digital yearbook bookcase Yearbook 2025 The printed version of the Rosehill College Yearbook 2025 is now available for a limited time. To place your order, please complete the online form using the link below. Once your payment has been received, your order will be processed and your printed Yearbook will be ready for collection from the Student Office. Be sure to get your copy before orders close — this is a great way to keep the memories of 2025 at Rosehill College! Order your copy now

title: School App Upgrade in Progress
description: We are upgrading the Rosehill College school phone app to provide a better and more reliable experience for our whānau, students, and staff.
text: Te taupānga kura Upgrade in progress We are currently upgrading our school phone app to provide a better and more reliable experience for our whānau, students, and staff. What's happening The current school app will be replaced with an improved version. More features and easier access. Instructions will be shared as soon as the new app is ready. Get the SchoolBridge app Need to reach us in the meantime? Report absences on 0800 722 929, or contact the front office for anything else. Contact us
